text: The video shows the laparoscopic approach to a D2 gastrectomy in an elderly patient, with visible growth near the antrum. The surgeon begins the procedure with a total omentectomy, starting near the mid portion of the transverse colon, where the tissue planes are opened up.

The surgeon divides the omental attachments towards the splenic flexure of the colon, freeing the omentum on the left side. The focus then shifts to the right side, performing omentectomy towards the hepatic flexure. The lesser sac is opened, and attachments between the pancreas and stomach are divided.

The surgeon continues dissection of the posterior attachments, revealing the pancreas. The dissection progresses close to the head of the pancreas, the pylorus, and the D1 junction, with the duodenum visible nearby.

The surgeon clips and dissects the right gastroepiploic vessel using an energy source, followed by the isolation and clipping of the right gastroepiploic artery with two proximal clips and one distal clip. Cold scissors are then used to divide the artery.
The surgeon creates a small window on the lesser curvature side, carefully dissecting small fibrotic adhesions while working near the gastroduodenal artery.
The surgeon performs hepatoduodenal nodal dissection, fully exposing the common bile duct and common hepatic duct while removing surrounding lymphovascular tissue.

The surgeon dissects from the anterior aspect of the common hepatic duct, revealing the hepatic artery just below the instrument. A bluish hue indicating the portal vein becomes visible, which will be demonstrated more clearly soon.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the hepatic artery and its gastroduodenal branch, noting an anatomical variation where the right and left hepatic arteries divide earlier than usual. Both arteries are seen running towards the liver. The right gastric artery is clipped and cut, similar to previous steps, while the surrounding lymphovascular tissue is exposed.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the vessels, ensuring they are completely bared with no residual tissue left behind. The ultrasonic shears are positioned with the negative part facing the vessels for precision. A bulky lymph node at the celiac axis level is completely excised, revealing the underlying vessel.

The surgeon exposes the hiatus, removing fatty tissue surrounding it and directing it towards the stomach. The esophagus is visible, and the duodenum is divided approximately 2.5 to 3 centimeters below. The gastroduodenal artery is clearly seen with visible pulsations, alongside the prehepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC).

The surgeon holds the tissues, revealing the splenic artery beneath the instrument. The coronary vein is ligated at its origin using two proximal clips and one distal clip, then divided with cold scissors. The left gastric artery becomes clearly visible as surrounding lymphovascular tissue is removed. The aorta comes into view, requiring careful dissection in this area.

The surgeon isolates a vessel, applying three proximal clips and one distal clip due to its direct connection to the aorta, indicating a high-pressure zone. An energy source is used to complement the clips for secure ligation.

The surgeon removes the final portion of lymphovascular tissue over the aorta, ensuring complete removal of lymphatic tissue. Dissection progresses towards the short gastric vessels, with the tip of the pancreas visible to the right of the instrument.

The spleen and stomach are visible in the surgical field, with the stomach positioned close to surrounding structures. The surgeon performs a comprehensive DII lymphadenectomy, ensuring all lymphovascular tissue is removed. The entire course of the splenic artery is exposed, along with the first branch of the short gastric artery.

In the subtotal gastrectomy procedure, the surgeon avoids splenic hilar dissection. The gastroduodenal artery and its branches, including the right and left hepatic arteries, are visible alongside the portal vein. A clip is applied to the right gastric artery near the celiac axis area. The transected duodenum and the hiatal region are clearly seen.

The surgeon clips the left gastric vein and artery, then dissects fatty tissue along the lesser curvature from the hiatus, leaving the area bare. The division site is marked, and the direction of the splenic short gastric vessels is noted, running cranially as a landmark. Below this, vessels run caudally. A green cartridge is used for stapling due to the stomach's thickness, crossing the previous staple line.

The surgeon ensures the staple line is secure before using cold scissors to divide between the four rows of staples, completing the division of the stomach. The gastrocolic vessel is clearly visible, confirming the correct surgical plane. The jejunum is measured at approximately forty centimeters from the duodenojejunal flexure, and a small enterotomy is made. A posterior gastrotomy is performed using ultrasonic shears, maintaining a distance of three centimeters from the staple line.

The surgeon introduces a staple to pull the tissues together for anastomosis, ensuring the staple gun is closed during retrieval to prevent a wide otomy. A stay suture is placed on the inferior aspect, followed by suturing from the superior aspect, with bites overlapping the enterotomy by approximately one centimeter for comprehensive closure.
The surgeon uses a PDS suture to close the enterotomies, beginning the suturing from the superior aspect. The suture technique is performed laparoscopically, ensuring precise closure of the tissue.

The surgeon uses a PDS suture to close the enterotomy between the stomach and jejunum, taking full-thickness bites on the stomach portion and minimal mucosa on the jejunum side. An assisting instrument holds the thread vertically for comfortable suturing, ensuring precise needle placement for a secure anastomosis. A rails tube is placed through the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon makes a small four-centimeter vertical incision in the upper midline to remove the entire specimen, including the omentum. The stomach is held manually to avoid damage, especially near the tumor site. The jejunojejunostomy is performed extracorporeally.
